cat > scratch/test_solve.py << 'EOF'
import torch, triton, triton.language as tl, numpy as np

@triton.jit
def _inv16(Nii, BC: tl.constexpr, P: tl.constexpr):
    A0 = -Nii
    offs = tl.arange(0, BC)
    for ii in range(1, BC):
        r_ii = (offs == ii)
        rvec = tl.sum(tl.where(r_ii[:, None], A0, 0.0), axis=0)
        contrib = tl.sum(rvec[:, None] * A0, axis=0)
        upd = r_ii[:, None] & (offs[None, :] < ii)
        A0 = tl.where(upd, A0 + contrib[None, :], A0)
    return tl.where(offs[:, None] == offs[None, :], 1.0, A0)

@triton.jit
def _blk4(N4, bi, bk, NB: tl.constexpr):
    sel = (tl.arange(0, NB)[:, None, None, None] == bi) & (tl.arange(0, NB)[None, None, :, None] == bk)
    return tl.sum(tl.sum(tl.where(sel, N4, 0.0), axis=0), axis=1)

@triton.jit
def _blkrow(M4, bi, NB: tl.constexpr):
    sel = (tl.arange(0, NB)[:, None, None] == bi)
    return tl.sum(tl.where(sel, M4, 0.0), axis=0)

@triton.jit
def blocked_w(N_ptr, b_ptr, w_ptr, BT: tl.constexpr, BC: tl.constexpr, K: tl.constexpr, NB: tl.constexpr, P: tl.constexpr):
    offs = tl.arange(0, BT); ok = tl.arange(0, K); ob = tl.arange(0, BC)
    N = tl.load(N_ptr + offs[:, None]*BT + offs[None, :])
    b = tl.load(b_ptr + offs[:, None]*K + ok[None, :])
    N4 = N.reshape(NB, BC, NB, BC); b4 = b.reshape(NB, BC, K)
    d0 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 0, 0, NB), BC, P); d1 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 1, 1, NB), BC, P)
    d2 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 2, 2, NB), BC, P); d3 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 3, 3, NB), BC, P)
    n10 = _blk4(N4, 1, 0, NB)
    n20 = _blk4(N4, 2, 0, NB); n21 = _blk4(N4, 2, 1, NB)
    n30 = _blk4(N4, 3, 0, NB); n31 = _blk4(N4, 3, 1, NB); n32 = _blk4(N4, 3, 2, NB)
    t10 = -tl.dot(d1, tl.dot(n10, d0, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    t20 = -tl.dot(d2, tl.dot(n20, d0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n21, t10, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    t21 = -tl.dot(d2, tl.dot(n21, d1, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    t30 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n30, d0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n31, t10, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n32, t20, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    t31 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n31, d1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n32, t21, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    t32 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n32, d2, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
    bg0=_blkrow(b4,0,NB); bg1=_blkrow(b4,1,NB); bg2=_blkrow(b4,2,NB); bg3=_blkrow(b4,3,NB)
    w0 = tl.dot(d0, bg0, input_precision=P)
    w1 = tl.dot(t10, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d1, bg1, input_precision=P)
    w2 = tl.dot(t20, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t21, bg1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d2, bg2, input_precision=P)
    w3 = tl.dot(t30, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t31, bg1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t32, bg2, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d3, bg3, input_precision=P)
    tl.store(w_ptr + (0*BC+ob)[:, None]*K + ok[None, :], w0)
    tl.store(w_ptr + (1*BC+ob)[:, None]*K + ok[None, :], w1)
    tl.store(w_ptr + (2*BC+ob)[:, None]*K + ok[None, :], w2)
    tl.store(w_ptr + (3*BC+ob)[:, None]*K + ok[None, :], w3)

BT=64; BC=16; K=128; NB=4
torch.manual_seed(0)
N = torch.randn(BT,BT,device='cuda')*0.2; N = torch.tril(N, diagonal=-1)
b = torch.randn(BT,K,device='cuda')*0.3
A0=-N.clone()
for i in range(1,BT):
    r=A0[i,:].clone(); A0[i,:i]=A0[i,:i].clone()+(r[:,None]*A0[:,:i]).sum(-2)
Tinv=torch.eye(BT,device='cuda')+A0; wref=Tinv@b
for P in ['tf32','tf32x3','ieee']:
    w = torch.empty(BT,K,device='cuda')
    blocked_w[(1,)](N, b, w, BT=BT, BC=BC, K=K, NB=NB, P=P, num_warps=4)
    print(f"P={P:7s} maxdiff={(w-wref).abs().max().item():.2e}  allclose(1e-4)={torch.allclose(w,wref,atol=1e-4)}")
EOF

stdout (1777 chars) — TRUNCATED
1	"""Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) forward, chunk form — custom Triton kernels for
2	SM120 (RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell).
3	
4	Two-kernel split:
5	
6	  1) intra kernel  — grid (B*H*NT,).  One program per (b, h, chunk).
7	     Computes, per chunk (in-chunk cumsummed gate g_cs):
8	        k_g  = k * exp(g_cs);  k_ng = k * exp(-g_cs);  q_g = q * exp(g_cs)
9	        gram = k_g @ k_ng^T                        (decayed K-K gram)
10	        N    = beta_row * gram                     (strictly lower)
11	        Tinv = (I + N)^{-1} = I + sum_{k>=1} (-N)^k   (Neumann row-scan)
12	        A    = Tinv * beta_col
13	        w    = A @ k_g         ;  u = A @ v
14	        Aqk  = lower_incl_diag(q_g @ k_ng^T)
15	     Stores w, u, Aqk, q_g, k_ng, g_last (=g_cs[BT-1]) to HBM.
16	
17	  2) recurrence kernel — grid (V/BV, B*H).  Sequential over chunks.
18	        v_i = u - w @ S
19	        o   = q_g @ S + Aqk @ v_i
20	        S   = exp(g_last) * (S + k_ng^T @ v_i)
21	
22	Moving the (sequential, expensive) tril solve out of the recurrence into the
23	embarrassingly-parallel intra kernel restores occupancy on the 240-SM GPU.
24	"""
25	from __future__ import annotations
26	
27	import torch
28	import torch.nn as nn
29	import triton
30	import triton.language as tl
31	
32	
33	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
34	# intra kernel: per (b, h, chunk)
35	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
36	@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B",

... (277 more chars)

33	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
34	# intra kernel: per (b, h, chunk)
35	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
36	@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B", "T", "H", "scale"])
37	def _kda_intra_kernel(
38	    q_ptr, k_ptr, v_ptr, g_ptr, beta_ptr,
39	    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr, qg_ptr, kng_ptr, glast_ptr,
40	    scale,
41	    B, T, H,
42	    NT: tl.constexpr,
43	    BT: tl.constexpr,
44	    K: tl.constexpr,
45	    V: tl.constexpr,
46	    BV: tl.constexpr,
47	    PREC: tl.constexpr,
48	):
49	    pid = tl.program_id(0)
50	    i_b = pid // (H * NT)
51	    rem = pid % (H * NT)
52	    i_h = rem // NT
53	    i_n = rem % NT
54	
55	    HK = H * K
56	    HV = H * V
57	
58	    offs_r = tl.arange(0, BT)
59	    offs_k = tl.arange(0, K)
60	    rr = offs_r[:, None]
61	    cc = offs_r[None, :]
62	
63	    t_idx = i_n * BT + offs_r
64	    qk_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HK + i_h * K
65	    v_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HV + i_h * V
66	
67	    k = tl.load(k_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32)
68	    q = tl.load(q_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32) * scale
69	    g = tl.load(g_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32)
70	    beta = tl.load(beta_ptr + (i_b * T + t_idx) * H + i_h).to(tl.float32)
71	
72	    g_cs = tl.cumsum(g, axis=0)
73	    g_last = tl.sum(g, axis=0)                       # [K] == g_cs[BT-1]
74	
75	    eg = tl.exp(g_cs)
76	    k_g = k * eg
77	    k

Edit /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass/solution.py
diff (41 lines)
--- a//workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass/solution.py
+++ b//workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass/solution.py
@@ -28,6 +28,38 @@
 import torch.nn as nn
 import triton
 import triton.language as tl
+
+
+# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
+# blocked tril-solve helpers (BT=64 split into NB=4 blocks of BC=16)
+# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
+@triton.jit
+def _inv16(Nii, BC: tl.constexpr):
+    """Inverse of I+Nii for a strictly-lower BC x BC tile, via row-scan."""
+    A0 = -Nii
+    offs = tl.arange(0, BC)
+    for ii in range(1, BC):
+        r_ii = (offs == ii)
+        rvec = tl.sum(tl.where(r_ii[:, None], A0, 0.0), axis=0)
+        contrib = tl.sum(rvec[:, None] * A0, axis=0)
+        upd = r_ii[:, None] & (offs[None, :] < ii)
+        A0 = tl.where(upd, A0 + contrib[None, :], A0)
+    return tl.where(offs[:, None] == offs[None, :], 1.0, A0)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _blk4(N4, bi, bk, NB: tl.constexpr):
+    """Extract the [BC,BC] block (bi,bk) from a [NB,BC,NB,BC] reshaped tile."""
+    sel = (tl.arange(0, NB)[:, None, None, None] == bi) & \
+          (tl.arange(0, NB)[None, None, :, None] == bk)
+    return tl.sum(tl.sum(tl.where(sel, N4, 0.0), axis=0), axis=1)
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _blkrow(M4, bi, NB: tl.constexpr):
+    """Extract block-row bi [BC,K] from a [NB,BC,K] reshaped tile."""
+    sel = (tl.arange(0, NB)[:, None, None] == bi)
+    return tl.sum(tl.where(sel, M4, 0.0), axis=0)
 
 
 # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #

@@ -113,36 +113,68 @@
     q_g = q * eg
 
     gram = tl.dot(k_g, tl.trans(k_ng), input_precision=PREC)
-    N = tl.where(rr > cc, gram, 0.0) * beta[:, None]
-    A0 = -N
-
-    # Neumann row-scan -> Tinv = I + sum_{k>=1} A0^k  (matches reference loop)
-    for ii in range(1, BT):
-        row_ii = (offs_r == ii)
-        rvec = tl.sum(tl.where(row_ii[:, None], A0, 0.0), axis=0)
-        contrib = tl.sum(rvec[:, None] * A0, axis=0)
-        upd = row_ii[:, None] & (offs_r[None, :] < ii)
-        A0 = tl.where(upd, A0 + contrib[None, :], A0)
-    Tinv = tl.where(rr == cc, 1.0, A0)
-    A = Tinv * beta[None, :]
-
-    w = tl.dot(A, k_g, input_precision=PREC)                  # [BT, K]
+    N = tl.where(rr > cc, gram, 0.0) * beta[:, None]            # strictly lower
+
+    # ---- blocked forward-substitution: Tinv = (I + N)^{-1} ----
+    # NB=4 diagonal 16x16 inverses, then off-diagonal blocks via matmul.
+    # w[bi] = sum_k Tinv[bi][k] @ (beta*k_g)[k]  (computed block-wise).
+    N4 = tl.reshape(N, (NB, BC, NB, BC))
+    bg = beta[:, None] * k_g                                     # [BT, K]
+    bg4 = tl.reshape(bg, (NB, BC, K))
+
+    d0 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 0, 0, NB), BC)
+    d1 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 1, 1, NB), BC)
+    d2 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 2, 2, NB), BC)
+    d3 = _inv16(_blk4(N4, 3, 3, NB), BC)
+    n10 = _blk4(N4, 1, 0, NB)
+    n20 = _blk4(N4, 2, 0, NB); n21 = _blk4(N4, 2, 1, NB)
+    n30 = _blk4(N4, 3, 0, NB); n31 = _blk4(N4, 3, 1, NB); n32 = _blk4(N4, 3, 2, NB)
+    P = PSOLVE
+    t10 = -tl.dot(d1, tl.dot(n10, d0, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+    t20 = -tl.dot(d2, tl.dot(n20, d0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n21, t10, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+    t21 = -tl.dot(d2, tl.dot(n21, d1, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+    t30 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n30, d0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n31, t10, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n32, t20, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+    t31 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n31, d1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(n32, t21, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+    t32 = -tl.dot(d3, tl.dot(n32, d2, input_precision=P), input_precision=P)
+
+    bg0 = _blkrow(bg4, 0, NB); bg1 = _blkrow(bg4, 1, NB)
+    bg2 = _blkrow(bg4, 2, NB); bg3 = _blkrow(bg4, 3, NB)
+    w0 = tl.dot(d0, bg0, input_precision=P)
+    w1 = tl.dot(t10, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d1, bg1, input_precision=P)
+    w2 = tl.dot(t20, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t21, bg1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d2, bg2, input_precision=P)
+    w3 = tl.dot(t30, bg0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t31, bg1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t32, bg2, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d3, bg3, input_precision=P)
+
+    ob = tl.arange(0, BC)
+    tl.store(w_ptr + (pid * BT + 0 * BC + ob)[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], w0)
+    tl.store(w_ptr + (pid * BT + 1 * BC + ob)[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], w1)
+    tl.store(w_ptr + (pid * BT + 2 * BC + ob)[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], w2)
+    tl.store(w_ptr + (pid * BT + 3 * BC + ob)[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], w3)
+
     Aqk_full = tl.dot(q_g, tl.trans(k_ng), input_precision=PREC)
     Aqk = tl.where(rr >= cc, Aqk_full, 0.0)
 
     base = pid * BT + offs_r                              # [BT]
-    tl.store(w_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], w)
     tl.store(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :], Aqk)
     tl.store(qg_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], q_g)
     tl.store(kng_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], k_ng)
     tl.store(glast_ptr + pid * K + offs_k, g_last)
 
-    # u = A @ v, tiled over V to keep shared memory bounded
+    # u = Tinv @ (beta*v), tiled over V; reuse the same Tinv blocks.
     for i_v in range(0, V, BV):
         offs_v = i_v + tl.arange(0, BV)
         v_tile = tl.load(v_ptr + v_row[:, None] + offs_v[None, :]).to(tl.float32)
-        u_tile = tl.dot(A, v_tile, input_precision=PREC)       # [BT, BV]
-        tl.store(u_ptr + base[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :], u_tile)
+        bv = beta[:, None] * v_tile                           # [BT, BV]
+        bv4 = tl.reshape(bv, (NB, BC, BV))
+        bv0 = _blkrow(bv4, 0, NB); bv1 = _blkrow(bv4, 1, NB)
+        bv2 = _blkrow(bv4, 2, NB); bv3 = _blkrow(bv4, 3, NB)
+        u0 = tl.dot(d0, bv0, input_precision=P)
+        u1 = tl.dot(t10, bv0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d1, bv1, input_precision=P)
+        u2 = tl.dot(t20, bv0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t21, bv1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d2, bv2, input_precision=P)
+        u3 = tl.dot(t30, bv0, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t31, bv1, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(t32, bv2, input_precision=P) + tl.dot(d3, bv3, input_precision=P)
+        tl.store(u_ptr + (pid * BT + 0 * BC + ob)[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :], u0)
+        tl.store(u_ptr + (pid * BT + 1 * BC + ob)[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :], u1)
+        tl.store(u_ptr + (pid * BT + 2 * BC + ob)[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :], u2)
+        tl.store(u_ptr + (pid * BT + 3 * BC + ob)[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :], u3)

cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
# update prof2 to pass new args and try different PSOLVE
cat > scratch/prof3.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_intra_kernel, _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; BV=64; scale=float(K)**-0.5; BC=16; NB=4
    w=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    u=torch.empty(NBH*BT*V,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    Aqk=torch.empty(NBH*BT*BT,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    qg=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    kng=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    glast=torch.empty(NBH*K,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    for PS in ["tf32","tf32x3"]:
        for nw in [4,8]:
            def intra():
                _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](q,k,v,g,beta,w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,scale,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,BC=BC,NB=NB,PREC="tf32",PSOLVE=PS,num_warps=nw,num_stages=1)
            try:
                ti=tt.do_bench(intra,warmup=10,rep=100)
                print(f"B={B} T={T} H={H} PS={PS} nw={nw}: intra={ti*1000:.1f}us",end="  ")
            except Exception as e: print(f"PS={PS} nw={nw} ERR {type(e).__name__}"); continue
            def rec():
                _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,64),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,torch.empty_like(v),B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=64,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=1)
            tr=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=100)
            print(f"rec={tr*1000:.1f}us")
EOF

Actually, let me first just tune the recurrence BV. The sweep was for shape2 only. Let me reconsider what BV to use across all shapes. The recurrence re-reads V-independent intermediates (w, Aqk, qg, kng) per V-tile. Smaller BV = more redundant reads but more blocks. Let me think about the recurrence more carefully. For shape2 (8 streams): - BV=64: 16 blocks, 649us - BV=32: 32 blocks, 386us - BV=16: 64 blocks, 285us For shape1 (16 streams): - BV=64: 32 blocks, 334us - smaller BV would give more blocks. For shape3 (4 streams): - BV=64: 8 blocks, 330us - BV=16: 32 blocks So smaller BV consistently helps. But the redundant reads grow. Let me check: is the recurrence memory-bound (redundant reads) or compute-bound? For shape2 BV=16, 285us. The redundant reads: per V-tile (8 tiles), read w,qg,kng (each 8*64*64*128*4=16.8MB) + Aqk (8*64*64*64*4=8.4MB) = ~59MB per tile × 8 = 472MB. At 1.8TB/s = 262us. So ~262us of the 285us is memory traffic from redundant reads! The recurrence is memory-bound on redundant V-independent reads. So to speed up the recurrence, I must REDUCE the redundant V-independent reads. Options: 1. Larger BV (fewer tiles, less redundancy) but fewer blocks. Tension. 2. Recompute V-independent data from inputs (q,k,g) which might L2-cache. But w, Aqk are derived; qg, kng from q,k,g. 3. The 2-pass approach: separate the state computation (needs w, u, kng, glast) from output (needs qg, Aqk, w, u). Hmm. Let me reconsider option 2: instead of storing qg, kng, store nothing extra and recompute in recurrence from q,k,g (which are inputs, L2-resident). But w and Aqk MUST be stored (they need the solve). So the recurrence reads w, Aqk (V-independent, must be stored) per tile regardless. w=16.8MB, Aqk=8.4MB → 25MB per tile. For BV=16 (8 tiles): 200MB → 111us. Plus u (V-dep) 16.8MB once. Better than 472MB but still significant. Actually, the cleanest fix for redundant reads: DON'T tile V in the recurrence. Use BV=V=128 (1 tile per stream). Then V-independent data read ONCE. But BV=128 failed earlier (shared mem) and gives few blocks (8-16). Hmm, the shared mem issue with BV=128: state S[K,BV]=[128,128]=64KB + other tiles. Its tiles: S[128,128]=64KB, w[64,128]=32KB, Aqk[64,64]=16KB, qg[64,128]=32KB, kng[64,128]=32KB, u[64,128]=32KB, glast[128]. As fp32 in shared... that's ~200KB. Way over 101KB. So BV=128 won't fit. The issue: holding all these fp32 tiles. The recurrence loads w,qg,kng,u,Aqk all as fp32. That's a lot. Idea: reduce the recurrence's memory footprint. The recurrence per chunk needs: - w [BT,K] for w@S - qg [BT,K] for qg@S - kng [BT,K] for kng^T@v_i - Aqk [BT,BT] for Aqk@v_i - u [BT,BV] for v_i=u-w@S - glast [K] for state scale What if I store these in bf16 instead of fp32? w, qg, kng are products ~ small magnitude, bf16 might lose precision but the recurrence accumulates... For small_qkv (5e-4), bf16 (1e-3 rel) is too coarse. Risky. What if I restructure to NOT hold all simultaneously? Process the recurrence chunk-by-chunk, loading one at a time. They're already loaded one chunk at a time in the loop. The shared mem is per-iteration tiles, not accumulated. So the 200KB estimate is wrong — Triton only needs the CURRENT iteration's tiles live. But it still allocates shared for the dot operands of the current iteration's dots. Let me reconsider: per iteration, the dots are w@S (w[64,128], S[128,BV]), qg@S, kng^T@v_i, Aqk@v_i. The operand tiles: w, qg, kng [64,128] each=32KB, Aqk[64,64]=16KB, u[64,BV], S[128,BV], v_i[64,BV]. For BV=128: S[128,128]=64KB, u[64,128]=32KB, v_i[64,128]=32KB. Plus w,qg,kng 3×32KB=96KB. Total operand shared ~ 64+32+32+96+16 = 240KB. Way over. The dominant cost is S [128,BV] and the three [64,128] tiles. To reduce: tile K. If I tile K (like FLA's h1,h2), then S is [BC_K, BV] = [64, BV] not [128,BV]. And w,qg,kng loaded in [64,64] tiles (BC_K=64). That halves the K-dimension tiles. FLA tiles K into 64-wide. For K=128, that's 2 K-tiles. The state h1 [64,BV], h2 [64,BV]. The dots loop over K-tiles. Let me reconsider: this K-tiling reduces shared mem AND register pressure, allowing larger BV or more blocks/SM. But adds a loop over 2 K-tiles per chunk. This is getting complicated. Let me step back and think about the recurrence more holistically. Actually — the BIGGEST insight: the recurrence re-reads V-independent data (w, qg, kng, Aqk) per V-tile. This is the memory bottleneck (262us of 285us for shape2 BV=16). The cleanest fix: **process the V-independent parts ONCE per chunk and the V-dependent parts per tile.** This is exactly what the 2-pass / FLA structure does: - Pass 1 (state, sequential): per chunk, needs w, u, kng, glast. w, kng, glast V-independent. - Pass 2 (output, parallel): per chunk, needs qg, Aqk, w, u, S_before. What if I DON'T V-tile the recurrence but INSTEAD get parallelism from the chunk dimension by separating state-computation (sequential) from output (parallel)? 2-pass plan: - Pass A (sequential, B*H blocks, BV=full V or tiled): compute states S_n. Per chunk: v_i=u-w@S, S=exp(glast)(S+kng^T@v_i). Store S_before[n]. ALSO this needs w (V-indep), u (V-dep), kng (V-indep), glast (V-indep). Re-reads w,qg,Aqk per tile. Hmm, both still V-tile-redundant. The fundamental issue: V-tiling ANY kernel that uses V-independent data causes redundant reads. Alternative: DON'T V-tile. Use BV=V. But then shared mem blows up (S[128,128]). Alternative: V-tile but keep V-independent data in L2 across tiles. For shape2, w+qg+kng+Aqk = 59MB < 96MB L2. If they stay in L2, the redundant reads are served from L2 (fast, ~3-4TB/s effective). The first tile reads from HBM (cold, but L2 flushed before call so HBM), subsequent tiles from L2. So effective HBM traffic ~1× (59MB) not 8×. But the timing showed 285us ≈ 262us memory (assuming HBM). If it were L2-served, it'd be ~59MB/4TB/s = 15us. So it's NOT L2-served. Why? Maybe the working set per chunk iteration evicts the V-independent data, or the access pattern doesn't reuse L2 well, or 59MB intermediates + 33MB inputs exceed L2 and thrash. Hmm. Let me reconsider. The intermediates are laid out as (B*H*NT, BT, D). The recurrence for stream (b,h) reads chunks 0..NT-1 sequentially. Need K-tiling. - Pass 2 (parallel, V-tiled): o = qg@S_before + Aqk@v_i. Reads qg, Aqk (redundant per tile), S_before, v_i (V-dep). Pass 1 no redundancy (BV=V). Hmm, 8 blocks is low occupancy. But no redundancy. Trade-off. This is really in the weeds. Let me take a different, more empirical approach: just try a few recurrence variants and measure. Let me prioritize: Variant 1: current recurrence, BV adaptive (16 for low-stream shapes). Quick win. Variant 2: 2-pass (sequential state + parallel output). Bigger change. Let me do Variant 1 first (just tune BV), measure, then decide on Variant 2. Actually, from the sweep, BV=16 warps=4 was best for shape2. Let me make BV adaptive: choose BV to target ~64+ blocks. BV = max(16, V // max(1, 128//(B*H)))? Let me just compute: target_blocks = B*H*(V/BV) >= 64. BV = V*8 / 64... Let me just set BV = 16 if B*H <= 8 else 32. Hmm. Let me think: - shape0: B*H=16 → BV=32 → 64 blocks. Or BV=16 → 128 blocks. - shape1: B*H=16 → BV=32 → 64 blocks. - shape2: B*H=8 → BV=16 → 64 blocks. - shape3: B*H=4 → BV=16 → 32 blocks. Let me just sweep BV per shape quickly and pick. Actually, the sweep takes long (compiles). Let me make BV adaptive and benchmark, trying a couple values. Actually, let me reconsider — there might be a much bigger win I'm missing. Let me reconsider the recurrence structure entirely. The recurrence is sequential because S_n depends on S_{n-1}. The output o_n depends on S_{n-1}. Total sequential depth = NT. But what if I use the PARALLEL SCAN over chunks? Earlier I dismissed it because v_i depends on S. But let me reconsider. The recurrence: S_n = exp(glast_n)*(S_{n-1} + kng_n^T @ v_i_n), v_i_n = u_n - w_n@S_{n-1}. S_n = exp(glast_n)*S_{n-1} + exp(glast_n)*kng_n^T@u_n - exp(glast_n)*kng_n^T@w_n@S_{n-1} = [exp(glast_n)*I - exp(glast_n)*kng_n^T@w_n] @ S_{n-1} + exp(glast_n)*kng_n^T@u_n = M_n @ S_{n-1} + C_n where M_n = exp(glast_n)*(I - kng_n^T@w_n) [K×K full], C_n = exp(glast_n)*kng_n^T@u_n [K×V]. M_n is K×K = 128×128 (full). C_n is K×V. Parallel scan: S_n = (Π_{j=1}^{n} M_j) S_0 + Σ_{j=1}^{n} (Π_{k=j+1}^{n} M_k) C_j. With S_0=0: S_n = Σ_{j=1}^{n} (Π_{k=j+1}^{n} M_k) C_j. The products of M_k are 128×128 matrix mults. The scan combines (M, C) pairs. For NT chunks, a Blelloch scan does O(NT log NT) combinations, each a 128×128×128 matmul (for M@M) and 128×128×V (for M@C). For NT=64: log2(64)=6 levels, each NT/2 combinations = 32. So ~6×32 = 192 combinations. Each 128×128×128 = 2M FMA (for M@M) + M@C. Total ~192×(2M + 0.5M) ≈ 480M FMA per stream. ×8 streams = 3.8 GFLOP. vs sequential recurrence ~6 GFLOP. Comparable. But the scan is PARALLEL within each level (32 combos in parallel) and only 6 sequential levels. So much better parallelism! But the M@M matmuls are 128×128×128 — these need to be done. And storing all M_n (NT × 128×128 = 64×16KB = 1MB per stream, 8MB total) and C_n (NT×128×128×4 = 33MB). Hmm, this is a significant rewrite but could be much faster (6 sequential levels vs 64). The matmuls are parallelizable. Actually, the scan approach is what FlashAttention-like linear attention recurrences sometimes use (e.g., for chunked linear attention with gates). But it's complex. And the M@M 128×128 matmuls per combination are real work. Let me estimate: 6 levels × 32 parallel M@M (128^3=2M each) + M@C. Per stream: 6 levels, but each level's 32 combos run in parallel. With 8 streams, level work = 8 streams × 32 combos = 256 parallel M@M. On 240 SMs, ~1 wave per level. Each M@M [128,128,128] ~ maybe 2us. 256/240 ≈ 1 wave → 2us/level × 6 levels = 12us for the scan. Plus M@C and the output. Plus computing M_n, C_n (parallel over chunks). That could be ~30-50us total for the recurrence vs 285us! 6-10x speedup. Worth trying. But it's a big rewrite and the M_n is K×K full which is a lot of intermediate storage (8MB for M, 33MB for C). And precision: accumulating matrix products over the scan could lose precision. M_n = exp(glast)*(I - kng^T@w). kng^T@w is [K,BT]@[BT,K]=[K,K]. Its magnitude? kng^T@w entries ~ Σ over BT of kng*w ~ 64 * 0.1*0.1*... could be O(1). I - that could have entries near 0 or 2. exp(glast)~0.05. So M_n entries ~ 0.05*(1 ± 1) ~ small. The product Π M_k over 64 chunks → tiny (each ~0.05). So S_n stays bounded. Precision: M products decay, so accumulation is stable. Might be OK. Hmm, but this is a LOT of work to implement and debug. Let me weigh: the 2-pass is simpler (~1.3x), the scan is complex (~6x potential). Given time constraints, let me first do the SIMPLE wins (BV tuning) and the 2-pass, measure, and if still far from target, consider the scan. Actually, let me reconsider the target. stdout (1316 chars)
210	        pid = nh_off + i_n
211	        base = pid * BT + offs_r                          # [BT]
212	        w = tl.load(w_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
213	        u = tl.load(u_ptr + base[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :])
214	        Aqk = tl.load(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :])
215	        qg = tl.load(qg_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
216	        kng = tl.load(kng_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
217	        glast = tl.load(glast_ptr + pid * K + offs_k)
218	
219	        v_i = u - tl.dot(w, S, input_precision=PREC)
220	        o = tl.dot(qg, S, input_precision=PREC) + tl.dot(Aqk, v_i, input_precision=PREC)
221	
222	        t_idx = i_n * BT + offs_r
223	        v_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HV + i_h * V
224	        tl.store(o_ptr + v_row[:, None] + offs_v[None, :], o.to(o_ptr.dtype.element_ty))
225	
226	        kn = tl.dot(tl.trans(kng), v_i, input_precision=PREC)      # [K, BV]
227	        S = tl.exp(glast)[:, None] * (S + kn)
228	
229	
230	def _kda_fwd(q, k, v, g, beta, scale, chunk_size=64):
231	    B, T, H, K = q.shape
232	    V = v.shape[-1]
233	    BT = chunk_size
234	    assert T % BT == 0
235	    NT = T // BT
236	    device, dtype = q.device, q.dtype
237	    NBH = B * H * NT
238	
239	    # fp32 intermediates; layout (B*H*NT, BT, D) flat

stdout (1086 chars)
240	    opts = dict(device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
241	    w = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
242	    u = torch.empty(NBH * BT * V, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
243	    Aqk = torch.empty(NBH * BT * BT, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
244	    qg = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
245	    kng = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
246	    glast = torch.empty(NBH * K, **opts)
247	    o = torch.empty_like(v)
248	
249	    PREC = "tf32"
250	    PSOLVE = "tf32"
251	    BV = 64 if V >= 64 else V
252	    BC = 16
253	    NB = BT // BC
254	
255	    _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](
256	        q, k, v, g, beta,
257	        w, u, Aqk, qg, kng, glast,
258	        scale, B, T, H,
259	        NT=NT, BT=BT, K=K, V=V, BV=BV, BC=BC, NB=NB, PREC=PREC, PSOLVE=PSOLVE,
260	        num_warps=4, num_stages=1,
261	    )
262	    _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V, BV), B * H)](
263	        w, u, Aqk, qg, kng, glast, o,
264	        B, T, H,
265	        NT=NT, BT=BT, K=K, V=V, BV=BV, PREC=PREC,
266	        num_warps=4, num_stages=1,
267	    )
268	    return o

Let me think about the recurrence more carefully before just tuning BV. The redundant read issue is fundamental. Let me reconsider. Actually, let me reconsider the whole thing. The key insight: the recurrence is memory-bound on redundant V-independent reads (262us of 285us for shape2 BV=16). So tuning BV only helps marginally (BV=16: 285us, already near the memory floor). To break through, I need to ELIMINATE redundant reads. The cleanest way: **don't V-tile the sequential part; use BV=V with K-tiling.** Let me implement a recurrence kernel with K-tiling so BV=V fits. With K-tiling (BK=64, so K=128 → 2 K-tiles), state S is split into S0[64,BV], S1[64,BV]. For BV=V=128: S0[64,128]+S1[64,128]. Per chunk: - v_i = u - w@S: w@S = w0@S0 + w1@S1 where w split into [64,64] tiles. 2 dots [64,64]@[64,128]→[64,128]. - o = qg@S + Aqk@v_i: qg@S = qg0@S0 + qg1@S1. 2 dots + Aqk@v_i. - S update: kng^T@v_i: kng [64,128] split [64,64], transpose [64,64], @v_i[64,128]→[64,128] into S0, S1. 2 dots. So 7 dots/chunk (vs 4 before), but BV=V (no redundant reads) and smaller dots. For shape2: 8 blocks (B*H), sequential 64 chunks. 8 blocks low occupancy but each chunk does 7 small dots. Hmm, 8 blocks is really low. The dots would be [64,64]@[64,128]. With 8 blocks on 240 SMs... each block uses few SMs. Bad occupancy. Alternatively K-tiling with BV=64 (16 blocks shape2): S0[64,64]+S1[64,64]. 16 blocks. Better. And no... wait BV=64 still V-tiles (2 tiles for V=128). So still redundant reads. K-tiling + BV=64 doesn't eliminate redundancy. The ONLY way to eliminate redundancy is BV=V (no V-tiling). Which needs K-tiling for shared mem. Which gives few blocks. So there's a fundamental tradeoff: BV=V (no redundancy, few blocks) vs BV< V (redundancy, more blocks). The sweep showed BV< V wins (more blocks). So redundancy is cheaper than low occupancy, OR the redundancy is partly L2-served. Hmm wait, if BV=16 gives 285us ≈ memory floor (262us HBM), and BV=V with K-tiling gives 8 blocks... let me estimate BV=V: 8 blocks, 64 chunks, 7 dots/chunk (K-tiled, [64,64]@[64,128]). Per dot ~64*64*128*2=1M FMA. Per chunk 7M. Per block 64*7M=448M. 8 blocks parallel → if each block sustains ~200 GFLOPS (low occupancy single block), 448M/200G = 2.2ms. Way worse. Low occupancy kills it. So BV< V (more blocks) is right. The recurrence is memory-bound on redundant reads at the optimal BV. To go faster, I must reduce redundant reads WITHOUT losing blocks. The 2-pass helps: it moves output dots to a parallel kernel, so the SEQUENTIAL kernel reads fewer V-independent things. Let me reconsider the 2-pass with the redundant-read lens: - Sequential pass (state): reads w, u, kng, glast per chunk. V-independent: w, kng, glast. V-tiled (BV) → redundant w, kng, glast per tile. But only 2 dots/chunk (lighter). - Parallel pass (output): reads qg, Aqk, S_before, v_i per chunk. Parallel over chunks → even if redundant per tile, it's spread across many blocks and L2-friendly. The sequential pass redundant reads: w+kng = 2×16.8MB per tile × 8 = 269MB → 149us (shape2). Plus glast small. So sequential pass ~149us memory floor + 2 dots compute. Better than 262us (current reads w+qg+kng+Aqk). So 2-pass roughly halves the sequential memory traffic. Worth it. But honestly, the parallel scan would be much better (6 levels vs 64 sequential). Let me seriously consider implementing the scan. Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the scan's memory. The scan needs M_n [K,K] per chunk = NT*K*K = 64*128*128 = 1M floats = 4MB per stream, 32MB for 8 streams. C_n [K,V] = same as storing states = 33MB. The scan combines these. Hmm, ordering: at chunk n, S_before[n] = current S. Compute v_i = u - w@S_before. Store S_before[n] and v_i[n]. Then update S → S_after (becomes S_before[n+1]). Stores S_before[n], v_i[n]. - Output kernel (parallel, B*H*NT*(V/BV) blocks): 2 dots/chunk. State kernel reads w (redundant per tile), u (V-tile), kng (redundant), glast (redundant). So redundant: state 269MB + output 201MB = 470MB. Similar to current (472MB). No improvement in HBM traffic! The 2-pass doesn't reduce redundant reads (same V-independent data, just split across two kernels). The benefit is parallelism (output) and lighter sequential (state 2 dots vs 4). The sweep showed BV=16 best. The current uses BV=64. Let me FIRST just switch to BV=16 (cheap, proven by sweep) and benchmark. Then do the recompute optimization. Actually, let me be smarter and make it depend on getting ~64 blocks: BV = max(16, V // (64 // B*H))... let me just do: target 64 blocks. blocks = B*H*(V/BV) ≈ 64. BV = B*H*V/64. - shape0,1: 16*128/64 = 32. BV=32. - shape2: 8*128/64 = 16. BV=16. - shape3: 4*128/64 = 8. BV=8. Hmm shape3 BV=8 (tiny). Let me cap BV>=16. So shape3 BV=16 (32 blocks). Let me do: BV = max(16, V // max(1, 96 // (B*H))). For B*H=16: 96//16=6, V//6=21→BV=max(16,21)... let me just hardcode per stream count: ``` streams = B*H BV = 16 if streams <= 8 else 32 ``` shape0,1 (16 streams): BV=32 → 64 blocks. shape2 (8): BV=16 → 64 blocks. shape3 (4): BV=16 → 32 blocks. Let me try that. Benchmark. Then I'll implement recompute to cut HBM. Let me update.

Plus Aqk 16, S 8, u/v_i 8 = 128KB. Over by 27KB. To fit: reduce to 2 K-tiles live simultaneously. K-tiling! If w, qg, kng are processed in K-tiles (BK=64), only one [64,64] tile live at a time. Then w0[64,64]=16, qg0=16, kng0=16, Aqk=16, S[128,16]=8, u/v_i=8 = 80KB. Fits! But K-tiling the recurrence means: S split into S0[64,BV], S1[64,BV]. w@S = w0@S0 + w1@S1 (loop 2 K-tiles). Similarly qg@S, kng^T@v_i. And recompute qg0, kng0 per K-tile from q,k,g_cs. But recompute needs g_cs [64,128] (full, for cumsum). So g_cs [64,128]=32KB is live. Plus q[64,128], k[64,128] for recompute. Hmm, those are full. Wait, can I compute g_cs once [64,128], then for each K-tile compute qg_slice = q_slice*exp(g_cs_slice)? q_slice [64,64], g_cs_slice [64,64]. Load q in [64,64] tiles. So I load q0[64,64], compute qg0 = q0*exp(g_cs0). Load g_cs fully [64,128] (for cumsum) but access slices. The g_cs [64,128]=32KB live throughout (needed for both K-tiles' exp). Plus per-tile q0,kng0 etc. Live: g_cs[64,128]=32, (per k-tile) q0[64,64]=16, k0=16, qg0=16, kng0=16, w0=16, Aqk=16, S0[64,16]=4, S1[64,16]=4, u[64,16]=4, v_i=4. Hmm ~32+16*6 + 16 = ~144KB. Still over because g_cs is 32KB and 6 small tiles. Ugh. This is really tight. The recompute fundamentally needs g_cs [64,128]=32KB live. Let me reconsider — maybe store g_cs (32KB, less than qg+kng=64KB+glast). Then recompute qg, kng from q,k,g_cs. But that's q,k,g_cs live = 3×32=96KB + others. Over. OK, I think recompute is not feasible without major restructuring (K-tiling everything). Let me REVERT to storing qg, kng, glast (original, 0.0362) and accept the HBM. Then optimize elsewhere. Let me revert the recompute changes. No, glast is tiny, storing it is negligible. The win was dropping qg+kng (64KB HBM/chunk/tile). Hmm. Alternatively: store kng (for state update) and Aqk and w, but recompute qg=q*exp(g_cs) — needs g_cs. Store g_cs. So store w, u, Aqk, kng, g_cs. Recompute qg from q,g_cs (q from input L2, g_cs stored). V-independent stored: w, Aqk, kng, g_cs = 32+16+32+32 = 112KB/chunk/tile. vs original w,qg,kng,Aqk,glast = 32+32+32+16+0.5 = 112.5KB. Same! No win. OK. The V-independent data is fundamentally ~112KB/chunk (w, qg, kng, Aqk). Can't reduce without recompute (which needs shared mem). Let me just revert and accept 0.0362, then optimize the intra and try the 2-pass / other ideas. Actually, hold on. Let me reconsider the recompute once more. The overshoot is 15KB. What if I reduce Aqk storage? Aqk is [64,64]=16KB. Could recompute Aqk = qg@kng^T (lower-incl-diag) in recurrence — that's a GEMM (524K FMA), more compute but saves 16KB shared (don't load Aqk) and 16KB HBM/chunk/tile. If I recompute Aqk instead of storing it: store w, u, qg, kng, glast. Recompute Aqk = mask(qg@kng^T). V-independent stored: w,qg,kng = 96KB + glast 0.5. vs original 112.5KB. Saves 16KB HBM. And shared: don't load Aqk (saves 16KB), but compute it (qg@kng^T dot → result [64,64]=16KB). Net shared similar. Hmm, marginal. Let me just REVERT to original (store all) and move on to bigger fish. Let me revert efficiently. Actually, let me reconsider — the cleanest path forward given all this analysis: The recurrence is HBM-bound on ~112KB/chunk/tile of V-independent data. With BV=16 (8 tiles), that's 112×8 = 896KB/chunk × NT chunks × B*H streams... for shape2: 896KB × 64 × 8 = 459MB → 255us at 1.8TB/s. Matches the ~285us observed. To reduce: fewer tiles (larger BV) but fewer blocks. The sweep found BV=16 optimal. So we're near the HBM floor for this structure. The ONLY structural fix is the parallel scan (reduces sequential length, changes memory pattern) or the 2-pass (moves some work to parallel). Given the complexity, let me revert to the working 0.0362 version and then implement the 2-pass recurrence carefully (it at least parallelizes the output, even if HBM is similar, the parallelism helps). Actually, the 2-pass doesn't reduce HBM. Hmm. Wait, actually, let me reconsider the 2-pass HBM. - Current single recurrence reads per tile: w, qg, kng, Aqk (V-indep) + u (V-dep). - 2-pass: state kernel reads w, kng, glast + u per tile; output kernel reads qg, Aqk + S_before, v_i per tile. Total V-indep read: state (w,kng) + output (qg,Aqk) = same w,qg,kng,Aqk. No reduction. Plus S_before, v_i extra. So 2-pass is WORSE on HBM. The only benefit is parallelism (output kernel parallel). So 2-pass helps only if the output kernel's parallelism outweighs its extra HBM. Given the recurrence is HBM-bound, adding HBM (S_before, v_i) might hurt. Hmm. So neither 2-pass nor recompute easily helps. The scan is the real structural fix. Let me reconsider the scan seriously. Actually, let me reconsider the recurrence HBM. The 112KB/chunk/tile × 8 tiles = the issue. The scan changes this: instead of each tile re-reading w,qg,kng,Aqk per chunk, the scan computes the state transition matrices M_n once (parallel over chunks, B*H*NT blocks) and then does a parallel scan. The M_n computation is parallel (no redundant reads). The scan combines M_n (K×K) which is small. Let me reconsider the scan memory: - Compute M_n, C_n per chunk (parallel, B*H*NT blocks): each block reads w, u, kng, qg for its chunk (once, no V-tiling redundancy if... hmm, M_n is K×K, C_n is K×V). - M_n = exp(glast)*(I - kng^T@w). kng^T@w = [K,BT]@[BT,K] = [K,K]. Needs kng [BT,K], w [BT,K]. - C_n = exp(glast)*kng^T@u = [K,BT]@[BT,V] = [K,V]. Needs kng, u. So per chunk: read w, kng (V-indep), u (V-dep). Compute M_n [K,K], C_n [K,V]. This is parallel over chunks — NO V-tiling redundancy (each chunk computed once, full V). B*H*NT blocks. Reads w, kng, u once per chunk. - Scan: combine (M_n, C_n) pairs. M_n is K×K=128×128=64KB, C_n is K×V=128×128=64KB. NT=64 chunks. Total M: 64×64KB=4MB/stream × 8 = 32MB. C: 33MB/stream × 8... wait C_n is per chunk [K,V], NT chunks → NT*K*V = 64*128*128 = 1M floats = 4MB/stream × 8 = 33MB. So M+C = 65MB. Plus the scan work. - Output: o_n = qg_n@S_before[n] + Aqk_n@(u_n - w_n@S_before[n]). S_before[n] from scan. Parallel over chunks. Reads qg, Aqk, w, u, S_before per chunk. The scan has its own HBM (M, C, S_before all ~33MB each). Total intermediates ~100MB+. Round-trips. Hmm. Might not be better than the current 255us. This is really hard to reason about without measuring. Let me take a totally different, pragmatic approach: Let me REVERT to the working 0.0362 version (store qg,kng,glast), and then try to OPTIMIZE WITHIN the current structure: 1. Tune num_warps, num_stages for both kernels (autotune). 2. Try BC=32 for the intra solve (fewer, larger blocks). 3. Try bf16 storage for some intermediates to cut HBM (with correctness check). Actually #3 is interesting. If I store qg, kng, w in bf16 instead of fp32, that halves their HBM (the dominant cost). bf16 has ~1e-3 relative precision. For the recurrence which accumulates... the small_qkv case needs 5e-4. bf16 inputs to the dots (then fp32 accumulate) — the relative error ~1e-3 per dot, accumulating over 64 chunks... might exceed 5e-4. If I store them as bf16 and load as bf16, the tl.dot with bf16 inputs uses bf16 tensor cores (fast) and fp32 accumulate. The input rounding to bf16 (1e-3 rel) is the error source. cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
# Revert recurrence to load qg/kng/glast, and intra to store them.
python3 - << 'PYEOF'
import re
s=open('solution.py').read()

# 1. intra signature: add back qg_ptr,kng_ptr,glast_ptr
s=s.replace(
"""    q_ptr, k_ptr, v_ptr, g_ptr, beta_ptr,
    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr,
    scale,""",
"""    q_ptr, k_ptr, v_ptr, g_ptr, beta_ptr,
    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr, qg_ptr, kng_ptr, glast_ptr,
    scale:""")

# 2. intra: recompute g_last and store qg/kng/glast
s=s.replace(
"""    g_cs = tl.cumsum(g, axis=0)

    eg = tl.exp(g_cs)""",
"""    g_cs = tl.cumsum(g, axis=0)
    g_last = tl.sum(g, axis=0)

    eg = tl.exp(g_cs)""")
s=s.replace(
"""    base = pid * BT + offs_r                              # [BT]
    tl.store(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :], Aqk)""",
"""    base = pid * BT + offs_r                              # [BT]
    tl.store(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :], Aqk)
    tl.store(qg_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], q_g)
    tl.store(kng_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :], k_ng)
    tl.store(glast_ptr + pid * K + offs_k, g_last)""")

# 3. rec signature
s=s.replace(
"""    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr, q_ptr, k_ptr, g_ptr, o_ptr,
    scale,
    B, T, H,""",
"""    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr, qg_ptr, kng_ptr, glast_ptr, o_ptr,
    B, T, H,""")
# rec do_not_specialize
s=s.replace('@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B", "T", "H", "scale"])\ndef _kda_rec_kernel(',
            '@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B", "T", "H"])\ndef _kda_rec_kernel(')

# 4. rec body: load qg/kng/glast instead of recompute
s=s.replace(
"""        w = tl.load(w_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
        u = tl.load(u_ptr + base[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :])
        Aqk = tl.load(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :])

        # recompute q_g, k_ng, g_last from L2-resident inputs (avoids materialising
        # the V-independent q_g/k_ng/g_last per V-tile -> ~2x less HBM traffic).
        t_idx = i_n * BT + offs_r
        qk_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HK + i_h * K
        q = tl.load(q_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32) * scale
        k = tl.load(k_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32)
        g = tl.load(g_ptr + qk_row[:, None] + offs_k[None, :]).to(tl.float32)
        g_cs = tl.cumsum(g, axis=0)
        qg = q * tl.exp(g_cs)
        kng = k * tl.exp(-g_cs)
        glast = tl.exp(tl.sum(g, axis=0))

        v_i = u - tl.dot(w, S, input_precision=PREC)
        o = tl.dot(qg, S, input_precision=PREC) + tl.dot(Aqk, v_i, input_precision=PREC)

        v_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HV + i_h * V
        tl.store(o_ptr + v_row[:, None] + offs_v[None, :], o.to(o_ptr.dtype.element_ty))

        kn = tl.dot(tl.trans(kng), v_i, input_precision=PREC)      # [K, BV]
        S = glast[:, None] * (S + kn)""",
"""        w = tl.load(w_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
        u = tl.load(u_ptr + base[:, None] * V + offs_v[None, :])
        Aqk = tl.load(Aqk_ptr + base[:, None] * BT + offs_r[None, :])
        qg = tl.load(qg_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
        kng = tl.load(kng_ptr + base[:, None] * K + offs_k[None, :])
        glast = tl.load(glast_ptr + pid * K + offs_k)

        v_i = u - tl.dot(w, S, input_precision=PREC)
        o = tl.dot(qg, S, input_precision=PREC) + tl.dot(Aqk, v_i, input_precision=PREC)

        t_idx = i_n * BT + offs_r
        v_row = (i_b * T + t_idx) * HV + i_h * V
        tl.store(o_ptr + v_row[:, None] + offs_v[None, :], o.to(o_ptr.dtype.element_ty))

        kn = tl.dot(tl.trans(kng), v_i, input_precision=PREC)      # [K, BV]
        S = tl.exp(glast)[:, None] * (S + kn)""")

# remove the now-unused HK line in rec
s=s.replace("    HV = H * V\n    HK = H * K\n","    HV = H * V\n")

# 5. launcher allocs + calls
s=s.replace(
"""    w = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
    u = torch.empty(NBH * BT * V, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
    Aqk = torch.empty(NBH * BT * BT

@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B", "T", "H", "scale"])
def _kda_intra_kernel(
    q_ptr, k_ptr, v_ptr, g_ptr, beta_ptr,
    w_ptr, u_ptr, Aqk_ptr, qg_ptr, kng_ptr, glast_ptr,
    scale,
    B, T, H,
    NT: tl.constexpr,

    BV: tl.constexpr,
    BC: tl.constexpr,
    NB: tl.constexpr,
    PREC: tl.constexpr,
    PSOLVE: tl.constexpr,
):
    pid = tl.program_id(0)

259	
260	    _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](
261	        q, k, v, g, beta,
262	        w, u, Aqk, qg, kng, glast,
263	        scale, B, T, H,
264	        NT=NT, BT=BT, K=K, V=V, BV=BV, BC=BC, NB=NB, PREC=PREC, PSOLVE=PSOLVE,
265	        num_warps=4, num_stages=1,
266	    )
267	    _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V, BV), B * H)](
268	        w, u, Aqk, qg, kng, glast, o,
269	        B, T, H,
270	        NT=NT, BT=BT, K=K, V=V, BV=BV, PREC=PREC,
271	        num_warps=4, num_stages=1,
272	    )
273	    return o
274	
275	
276	class Model(nn.Module):

239	    # fp32 intermediates; layout (B*H*NT, BT, D) flat.
240	    # Only w, u, Aqk are materialised — q_g/k_ng/g_last are recomputed in the
241	    # recurrence from the (L2-resident) q,k,g inputs.
242	    opts = dict(device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
243	    w = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
244	    u = torch.empty(NBH * BT * V, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
245	    Aqk = torch.empty(NBH * BT * BT, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
246	    qg = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
247	    kng = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
248	    glast = torch.empty(NBH * K, **opts)
249	    o = torch.empty_like(v)
250	
251	    PREC = "tf32"
252	    PSOLVE = "tf32"
253	    # recurrence V-tile: target enough blocks to fill the GPU; smaller BV for

255	    streams = B * H

257	    BC = 16
258	    NB = BT // BC

@@ -1,11 +1,10 @@
-    # fp32 intermediates; layout (B*H*NT, BT, D) flat.
-    # Only w, u, Aqk are materialised — q_g/k_ng/g_last are recomputed in the
-    # recurrence from the (L2-resident) q,k,g inputs.
-    opts = dict(device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
-    w = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
-    u = torch.empty(NBH * BT * V, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
-    Aqk = torch.empty(NBH * BT * BT, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
-    qg = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
-    kng = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, **opts)
-    glast = torch.empty(NBH * K, **opts)
+    # Intermediates laid out flat as (B*H*NT, BT, D).
+    # V-independent w/q_g/k_ng/A_qk stored in bf16 to halve HBM traffic (the
+    # recurrence re-reads them per V-tile); compute stays fp32/tf32.
+    w = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
+    u = torch.empty(NBH * BT * V, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
+    Aqk = torch.empty(NBH * BT * BT, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
+    qg = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
+    kng = torch.empty(NBH * BT * K, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
+    glast = torch.empty(NBH * K, device=device, dtype=torch.float32)
     o = torch.empty_like(v)

0.0397 (from 0.0362). bf16 intermediates helped (halved V-independent HBM). Times improved: - shape0: 0.264ms (frac 0.0406) - shape1: 0.407ms (frac 0.0528) - shape2: 0.477ms (frac 0.0450) - shape3: 0.210ms (frac 0.0256) Still the recurrence dominates. Let me update my task tracking and think about the next big lever. Current state: 0.0397. Let me re-profile intra vs rec. Let me save progress to memory first (this is a long task), then continue. Actually, let me keep going — let me re-profile to see current intra/rec split. Let me think about what's left: 1. The recurrence is still HBM-bound on V-independent reads (now bf16, ~half). 2. The intra is still ~245us (blocked solve + dots). For the recurrence, the bf16 halved HBM but it's still the bottleneck. Let me reconsider the 2-pass or scan now that bf16 reduced HBM. Actually, let me reconsider — with bf16, the V-independent HBM is now: w(16KB)+qg(16KB)+kng(16KB)+Aqk(8KB) = 56KB/chunk/tile (bf16). × 8 tiles × 64 chunks × 8 streams (shape2) = 229MB → 127us. Plus u (bf16, V-dep) and glast. The recurrence for shape2 is now ~? Let me profile. Actually let me reconsider the whole approach. I keep nibbling. Let me think bigger. The fundamental issue: the recurrence re-reads V-independent data per V-tile. Even at bf16, with 8 tiles that's 8x the V-independent data. What if I reduce the number of tiles by using larger BV but compensating occupancy another way? The issue with larger BV is fewer blocks. But what if I make each block do MORE chunks (persistent) or process multiple streams? Hmm. Actually, let me reconsider: the sweep showed BV=16 best for shape2 (64 blocks). With bf16 now, maybe BV=32 (32 blocks) is competitive (less redundancy). Let me re-sweep BV with bf16. Actually, let me reconsider a cleaner idea: **fuse the recurrence to NOT re-read V-independent data per tile by processing all V-tiles of a chunk together in one block, sharing the V-independent loads via shared memory.** I.e., one block per (b,h) stream, loading w/qg/kng/Aqk ONCE per chunk into shared, then computing all V-tiles. But that's BV=V (no V-tiling) which has the shared mem problem (S[128,V]). Unless... I tile V inside the block, reusing the shared V-independent data. So one block per (b,h), loads w/qg/kng/Aqk per chunk into registers/shared ONCE, then loops over V-tiles computing each. The V-independent data read once (no redundancy!). The state S[128,V] is the issue — but if I process V in tiles within the block, S[K, BV] for BV=16, and I keep SEPARATE S per V-tile... no, the recurrence state S is per V-slice and persists across chunks. So for BV=16, 8 V-slices, each with its own S[128,16]. A single block would need to maintain 8 states (8×[128,16]=8×8KB=64KB) AND loop. That's a lot per block. Hmm. With B*H blocks (8-16), each maintaining 8 V-slice states and processing all chunks. Low block count (8-16) but no redundant reads. The per-block work: 8 V-slices × NT chunks × dots. For shape2: 8 streams, each block does 8 slices × 64 chunks. Total work same, but only 8 blocks (low occupancy). The trade-off: no redundancy (good) vs low occupancy (8 blocks). For shape2, 8 blocks on 240 SMs = 3% occupancy. Probably bad. OK. Let me reconsider. The redundant read is fundamental to V-tiling. The bf16 halved it. Further reduction needs recompute (shared mem issue) or the scan. Let me seriously consider the scan now, OR accept current and optimize intra + tune. Actually, let me reconsider the numbers. Current 0.0397. To get to 0.1 I need ~2.5x. The recurrence is ~half the time. If I halve the recurrence (via scan or 2-pass), I get ~1.5x overall → ~0.06. To hit 0.1 I'd ALSO need to halve intra. Let me look at the intra. 245us for shape2 (512 blocks). Per block: gram(524K) + solve(~25 dots) + w(4 dots) + Aqk(524K) + u(8 dots). Lots of dots. The inv16 (4×16-scan) and ~25 block dots. Hmm, the blocked solve has many small [16,16]@[16,K] dots. These might be inefficient (small M=16). Let me reconsider BC. With BC=32 (NB=2): fewer blocks (2), larger dots [32,32]@[32,K]. The inv32 (32-scan) is slower but fewer. Let me test BC=32. Actually, let me reconsider the whole intra efficiency. Let me profile what fraction is the solve vs the GEMMs (gram, w, u, Aqk). Let me re-profile intra/rec split and also think about num_warps tuning (autotune). Let me write a quick profile. Actually, let me be more strategic. Let me use Triton's autotuner for both kernels to find good configs (num_warps, num_stages, BC) automatically. The benchmark does 10 warmups which absorb autotune. This is more efficient than manual sweeping. Let me add @triton.autotune to the intra and rec kernels. But autotune compiles many configs (slow first time). The benchmark/check call it ~9-20 times per shape; autotune cache persists across calls in a process. check.py runs all shapes in one process → autotune per shape. Should be OK. Actually, autotune with retargeting... let me reconsider. The key params: - intra: num_warps ∈ {4,8}, BC ∈ {16,32}, num_stages ∈ {1,2}, PSOLVE ∈ {tf32}. - rec: num_warps ∈ {2,4,8}, BV ∈ {16,32}, num_stages ∈ {1,2}. Let me add autotune. But I worry about compile time in check.py (many configs × 4 shapes). Let me limit configs. Hmm, actually, let me reconsider. The check.py runs once per shape with 3 seeds × 3 cases = 9 forward calls per shape. If autotune tries 8 configs, the first call per shape triggers autotune (8 compiles + timings). That's slow but OK (check.py isn't timed for score). The benchmark does 10 warmup + 20 timed; autotune in warmup. But the autotune key must include shape params so it re-tunes per shape. Let me set key=["BT","K","V"] (B,T,H change per shape but BT,K,V constant → might not re-tune). Hmm, I want per-(B,H) tuning for BV. Let me include B,H in key... but they're runtime (do_not_specialize). Autotune key uses arg names. Let me key on the constexprs and maybe a derived "streams" constexpr. This is getting complex. Let me just manually pick good configs based on a focused sweep, rather than autotune. Let me sweep BC for intra and BV/num_warps for rec with the bf16 version. Actually, let me step back. I've made good progress (0.004 → 0.0397, 10x). Let me save state to memory in case of context loss, then continue optimizing methodically. Let me first record key learnings to memory. Actually, the memory dir is for cross-session facts. This is a single task. Let me skip memory for now and focus on the optimization. I'll save a checkpoint note if needed. command (1620 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/prof4.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_intra_kernel, _kda_rec_kernel, _kda_fwd
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":2,"T":1024,"H":8},{"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; BV=16; scale=float(K)**-0.5; BC=16; NB=4
    w=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    u=torch.empty(NBH*BT*V,device=device,dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    Aqk=torch.empty(NBH*BT*BT,device=device,dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    qg=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    kng=torch.empty(NBH*BT*K,device=device,dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    glast=torch.empty(NBH*K,device=device,dtype=torch.float32)
    def intra(): _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](q,k,v,g,beta,w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,scale,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,BC=BC,NB=NB,PREC="tf32",PSOLVE="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=1)
    def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,BV),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,torch.empty_like(v),B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=1)
    ti=tt.do_bench(intra,warmup=10,rep=100); tr_=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=100)
    print(f"B={B} T={T} H={H}: intra={ti*1000:.0f}us rec={tr_*1000:.0f}us")
EOF

For bi=3: k=0,1,2,3, 4 dots ×16 = 64 contraction. So u3 = 4 dots × [16,16]@[16,BV] = 4×16×16×BV. For BV=16: 4×16×16×16 = 16K FMA. u3 covers [16(BT)]×[BV] output = 16×16=256 outputs, each sum over 64 → 256×64 = 16K. ✓. So u3 = 16K FMA. command (1364 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/prof5.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_intra_kernel, _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
shape={"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8,"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64}
for k,v in shape.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
q,k,v,g,beta=inp
B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; scale=float(K)**-0.5
def mk(dtype,size): return torch.empty(size,device=device,dtype=dtype)
sz_w=NBH*BT*K; sz_u=NBH*BT*V; sz_A=NBH*BT*BT; sz_g=NBH*K
for BC in [16,32]:
  NB=BT//BC
  for BVU in [64,128]:
    for nw in [4,8]:
      w=mk(torch.bfloat16,sz_w); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,sz_u); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,sz_A)
      qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,sz_w); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,sz_w); glast=mk(torch.float32,sz_g)
      try:
        def f(): _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](q,k,v,g,beta,w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,scale,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BVU,BC=BC,NB=NB,PREC="tf32",PSOLVE="tf32",num_warps=nw,num_stages=1)
        t=tt.do_bench(f,warmup=10,rep=80)
        print(f"BC={BC} BVU={BVU} nw={nw}: intra={t*1000:.0f}us")
      except Exception as e: print(f"BC={BC} BVU={BVU} nw={nw}: ERR {type(e).__name__}")
EOF

command (1511 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/prof6.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_intra_kernel, _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":2,"T":1024,"H":8},{"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; scale=float(K)**-0.5; BC=16; NB=4
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    def intra(): _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](q,k,v,g,beta,w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,scale,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=V,BC=BC,NB=NB,PREC="tf32",PSOLVE="tf32",num_warps=8,num_stages=1)
    def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,16),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,torch.empty_like(v),B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=16,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=1)
    ti=tt.do_bench(intra,warmup=10,rep=100); tr_=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=100)
    print(f"B={B} T={T} H={H}: intra={ti*1000:.0f}us rec={tr_*1000:.0f}us  sum={ti*1000+tr_*1000:.0f}us")
EOF

command (1438 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/sweep_rec2.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; scale=float(K)**-0.5
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    o=torch.empty_like(v)
    print(f"--- B={B} T={T} H={H} ---")
    for BV in [16,32,64]:
        for nw in [2,4,8]:
            try:
                def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,BV),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,PREC="tf32",num_warps=nw,num_stages=1)
                t=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=80)
                print(f"  BV={BV} nw={nw}: {t*1000:.0f}us  blocks={triton.cdiv(V,BV)*B*H}")
            except Exception as e: print(f"  BV={BV} nw={nw}: ERR {type(e).__name__}")
EOF

Output pass reads qg, Aqk (V-indep) redundantly per tile. Same total V-independent reads. BUT the output pass is parallel (many blocks), so its HBM reads are spread across blocks and L2-friendly. args
{
  "command": "<1142 chars \u2014 see below>",
  "description": "Test if chunk_size affects result"

command (1142 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/test_chunksize.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
import reference
device='cuda:0'
torch.manual_seed(42); torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42)
B,T,H,K,V=2,2048,8,128,128
q=torch.randn(B,T,H,K,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)*0.1
k=torch.randn(B,T,H,K,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)*0.1
v=torch.randn(B,T,H,V,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device)*0.1
g=torch.randn(B,T,H,K,dtype=torch.float32,device=device)*0.1-0.05
beta=torch.sigmoid(torch.randn(B,T,H,dtype=torch.bfloat16,device=device))
scale=K**-0.5
o64 = reference._naive_chunk_kda(q,k,v,g,beta,scale,chunk_size=64)
o128 = reference._naive_chunk_kda(q,k,v,g,beta,scale,chunk_size=128)
o32 = reference._naive_chunk_kda(q,k,v,g,beta,scale,chunk_size=32)
diff128=(o64.float()-o128.float()).abs().max().item()
diff32=(o64.float()-o32.float()).abs().max().item()
print(f"chunk 64 vs 128: max_abs_diff={diff128:.3e}")
print(f"chunk 64 vs 32:  max_abs_diff={diff32:.3e}")
print("128 matches 64 (5e-2):", torch.allclose(o64.float(),o128.float(),atol=5e-2,rtol=5e-2))
EOF
uv run python scratch/test_chunksize.py 2>&1 | tail -5

command (1355 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/sweep_ns.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    o=torch.empty_like(v)
    print(f"--- B={B} T={T} H={H} ---")
    for ns in [1,2,3]:
      for BV in [16,32]:
        try:
            def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,BV),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=ns)
            t=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=80)
            print(f"  BV={BV} ns={ns}: {t*1000:.0f}us")
        except Exception as e: print(f"  BV={BV} ns={ns}: ERR {type(e).__name__}")
EOF

command (1652 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/prof7.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_intra_kernel, _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":2,"T":1024,"H":8},{"B":2,"T":2048,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT; scale=float(K)**-0.5; BC=16; NB=4
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    for ins in [1,2]:
      try:
        def intra(): _kda_intra_kernel[(NBH,)](q,k,v,g,beta,w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,scale,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=V,BC=BC,NB=NB,PREC="tf32",PSOLVE="tf32",num_warps=8,num_stages=ins)
        ti=tt.do_bench(intra,warmup=10,rep=80)
      except Exception as e: ti=f"ERR {type(e).__name__}"
      print(f"B={B} T={T} H={H} intra(ns={ins})={ti if isinstance(ti,str) else round(ti*1000)}us", end="  ")
    def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,16),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,torch.empty_like(v),B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=16,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=2)
    tr_=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=80)
    print(f"rec={tr_*1000:.0f}us")
EOF

command (1433 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/sweep_nw2.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4},{"B":2,"T":1024,"H":8}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    o=torch.empty_like(v)
    print(f"--- B={B} T={T} H={H} ---")
    for BV in [16,32]:
        for nw in [2,4,8]:
            for ns in [1,2]:
              try:
                def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,BV),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,PREC="tf32",num_warps=nw,num_stages=ns)
                t=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=80)
                print(f"  BV={BV} nw={nw} ns={ns}: {t*1000:.0f}us")
              except Exception as e: print(f"  BV={BV} nw={nw} ns={ns}: ERR")
EOF

command (1478 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/sweep_nw3.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
from solution import _kda_rec_kernel
import triton, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference
for shape in [{"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8},{"B":1,"T":2048,"H":4}]:
    sh={"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64,**shape}
    for k,v in sh.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
    init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
    q,k,v,g,beta=inp
    B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT
    def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
    w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
    qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
    o=torch.empty_like(v)
    print(f"--- B={B} T={T} H={H} ---")
    best=(9e9,None)
    for BV in [16,32]:
        for nw in [2,4,8]:
            for ns in [1,2]:
              try:
                def rec(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,BV),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=BV,PREC="tf32",num_warps=nw,num_stages=ns)
                t=tt.do_bench(rec,warmup=10,rep=80)
                if t*1000<best[0]: best=(t*1000,(BV,nw,ns))
                print(f"  BV={BV} nw={nw} ns={ns}: {t*1000:.0f}us")
              except Exception: pass
    print(f"  BEST: {best}")
EOF

V-independent bf16 HBM per chunk per tile: w(16)+qg(16)+kng(16)+Aqk(8)=56KB. ×8 tiles×64 chunks×8 streams=229MB → 127us. rec=171us. So ~74% HBM (127/171). Still partly HBM bound. Hmm. So there's still HBM headroom issue. The redundant V-independent reads (229MB) are 127us. If I could cut that... Wait — I tried recompute (didn't fit shared mem). Let me reconsider with ns=2 (more shared mem used). Recompute needs q,k,g,g_cs tiles. With ns=2 it'd be worse. Let me reconsider — what if I store ONLY w, u, Aqk (drop qg, kng, glast) and recompute qg, kng, glast from q,k,g, BUT make the recurrence fit shared mem by tiling K? The recompute needs: load q,k,g [64,128] each, cumsum g→g_cs [64,128], qg=q*exp(g_cs), kng=k*exp(-g_cs), glast=exp(sum g). That's q,k,g,g_cs tiles (4×32KB=128KB if fp32, or load bf16 q,k and fp32 g). To fit, I need to NOT hold all simultaneously. The dots need qg, kng, w (all [64,128]). command (1844 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/test_join.py << 'EOF'
import torch, triton, triton.language as tl
@triton.jit
def k(a_ptr,b_ptr,o_ptr, BT:tl.constexpr, K:tl.constexpr):
    ar=tl.arange(0,BT); ac=tl.arange(0,K)
    a=tl.load(a_ptr+ar[:,None]*K+ac[None,:])  # [BT,K]
    b=tl.load(b_ptr+ar[:,None]*K+ac[None,:])  # [BT,K]
    j=tl.join(a,b)   # ?
    tl.store(o_ptr + tl.arange(0,2*BT*2*K)[:,None], 0)  # noop
    # store j flattened
    off=tl.arange(0, 2*BT*K) if False else tl.arange(0,BT)
# just print shapes via error
try:
    a=torch.ones(64,128,device='cuda'); b=torch.ones(64,128,device='cuda')*2
    o=torch.empty(64*128*4,device='cuda')
    k[(1,)](a,b,o,BT=64,K=128,num_warps=4)
except Exception as e:
    print("err",e)

# Better: test the actual combine we want
@triton.jit
def k2(a_ptr,b_ptr,s_ptr,o_ptr, BT:tl.constexpr, K:tl.constexpr, BV:tl.constexpr):
    ar=tl.arange(0,BT); ac=tl.arange(0,K); av=tl.arange(0,BV)
    a=tl.load(a_ptr+ar[:,None]*K+ac[None,:])  # [BT,K] "w"
    b=tl.load(b_ptr+ar[:,None]*K+ac[None,:])  # [BT,K] "qg"
    s=tl.load(s_ptr+ac[:,None]*BV+av[None,:]) # [K,BV]
    # combine a,b along M: [2BT, K]
    ab=tl.join(a,b)            # [BT,K,2]
    ab=tl.permute(ab,(2,0,1))  # [2,BT,K]
    ab=tl.reshape(ab,(2*BT,K)) # [2BT,K]
    out=tl.dot(ab, s)          # [2BT, BV]
    oar=tl.arange(0,2*BT)
    tl.store(o_ptr+oar[:,None]*BV+av[None,:], out)
a=torch.randn(64,128,device='cuda'); b=torch.randn(64,128,device='cuda')
s=torch.randn(128,16,device='cuda'); o=torch.empty(128,16,device='cuda')
k2[(1,)](a,b,s,o,BT=64,K=128,BV=16,num_warps=4)
ref=torch.cat([a@s, b@s],dim=0)
print("combine match:", torch.allclose(o,ref,atol=1e-3), "maxdiff",(o-ref).abs().max().item())
print("o[0:4,0]",o[:4,0].cpu(), "a@s[0:4,0]",(a@s)[:4,0].cpu())
EOF
uv run python scratch/test_join.py 2>&1 | tail -5

Final tuning/robustness. Let me implement the intra full-A assembly. Assemble A [64,64] from blocks, then w=A@bg, u=A@bv as 2 big dots. Let me write it. The assembly: build A4 [NB,BC,NB,BC] from the blocks, reshape to [BT,BT]. ``` A4 = zeros(NB,BC,NB,BC) # place diagonal and off-diag blocks def place(A4, V, bi, bk): sel = (arange(NB)[:,None,None,None]==bi) & (arange(NB)[None,None,:,None]==bk) return A4 + where(sel, V[None,:,None,:], 0.0) A4 = place(A4, d0, 0,0); A4=place(A4,t10,1,0); A4=place(A4,d1,1,1) A4 = place(A4,t20,2,0); A4=place(A4,t21,2,1); A4=place(A4,d2,2,2) A4 = place(A4,t30,3,0); A4=place(A4,t31,3,1); A4=place(A4,t32,3,2); A4=place(A4,d3,3,3) A = A4.reshape(BT, BT) w = tl.dot(A, bg) # [BT,BT]@[BT,K] -> [BT,K] # u per V-tile u_tile = tl.dot(A, bv) # [BT,BT]@[BT,BV] -> [BT,BV] ``` The `place` does a where+add per block (10 blocks). Then reshape + 2 big dots. This replaces the block-wise w0..w3 (4 dots [16,16]@[16,128]) and u0..u3 (4 dots) with assembling (10 where+adds) + 2 big dots ([64,64]@[64,128]). The where+adds on [NB,BC,NB,BC]=[4,16,4,16]=4096 elements, 10 of them = 40K ops. Cheap. The 2 big dots vs 8 small dots: big dots more efficient. Net win. Let me implement. But wait — does the reshape A4[4,16,4,16]→[64,64] give the right layout? A4[bi,ri,bk,rk] → row bi*16+ri, col bk*16+16rk? reshape merges (0,1)→row and (2,3)→col. row index = bi*16+ri (since dim0 size 4, dim1 size 16: row = bi*16+ri). col = bk*16+rk. So A[row=bi*16+ri, col=bk*16+rk] = A4[bi,ri,bk,rk]. ✓. That's the block layout. Correct. Let me implement. Replace the block-wise w/u computation in the intra kernel. Let me write it. I'll replace from `bg0 = _blkrow...` through the u stores. Actually, this is a moderately big edit. Let me reconsider if it's worth it. intra=93us → maybe 70us? For shape2 total 264→241. Marginal. The recurrence is at its tuned optimum. To improve it, structural changes (scan, recompute) which are risky/marginal. Let me reconsider — maybe I should just accept ~0.08 and ensure it's robust. But let me make one more attempt at the recurrence via the parallel scan, since it's the only structural lever left and could give a real step-change. Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the recurrence HBM once more. The redundant reads (336MB shape2) are HBM. What if I REDUCE the number of V-tiles by using a larger BV but compensate with a 2D grid that adds chunk-parallelism for the OUTPUT only (2-pass)? The 2-pass: state (sequential, BV=16, 64 blocks) + output (parallel, larger BV). The output pass with BV=64 (efficient dots) and B*H*NT*(V/64) blocks = 8*64*2 = 1024 blocks (shape2). The output pass reads qg, Aqk, S_before, v_i. S_before, v_i are V-dependent. qg, Aqk V-independent but read in the parallel output pass. Hmm, the output pass still redundantly reads qg, Aqk per V-tile (2 tiles for BV=64). But it's parallel (1024 blocks). And the state pass (the sequential bottleneck) reads w, kng, glast, u — fewer V-independent (no qg, Aqk). command (2948 chars)
cd /workspace/problems/02_kda_cutlass
cat > scratch/test_combined.py << 'EOF'
import sys, torch
sys.path.insert(0,'.')
import triton, triton.language as tl, triton.testing as tt
device='cuda:0'
import reference

@triton.jit(do_not_specialize=["B","T","H"])
def rec_combined(w_ptr,u_ptr,Aqk_ptr,qg_ptr,kng_ptr,glast_ptr,o_ptr,B,T,H,
    NT:tl.constexpr,BT:tl.constexpr,K:tl.constexpr,V:tl.constexpr,BV:tl.constexpr,PREC:tl.constexpr):
    i_v=tl.program_id(0); i_nh=tl.program_id(1); i_b=i_nh//H; i_h=i_nh%H
    offs_r=tl.arange(0,BT); offs_k=tl.arange(0,K); offs_v=i_v*BV+tl.arange(0,BV)
    S=tl.zeros([K,BV],dtype=tl.float32); HV=H*V; nh_off=i_nh*NT
    nb2=tl.arange(0,2)
    for i_n in range(0,NT):
        pid=nh_off+i_n; base=pid*BT+offs_r
        w=tl.load(w_ptr+base[:,None]*K+offs_k[None,:]).to(tl.float32)
        u=tl.load(u_ptr+base[:,None]*V+offs_v[None,:]).to(tl.float32)
        Aqk=tl.load(Aqk_ptr+base[:,None]*BT+offs_r[None,:]).to(tl.float32)
        qg=tl.load(qg_ptr+base[:,None]*K+offs_k[None,:]).to(tl.float32)
        kng=tl.load(kng_ptr+base[:,None]*K+offs_k[None,:]).to(tl.float32)
        glast=tl.load(glast_ptr+pid*K+offs_k)
        # combine w@S + qg@S
        wq=tl.join(w,qg)             # [BT,K,2]
        wq=tl.permute(wq,(2,0,1))     # [2,BT,K]
        wq=tl.reshape(wq,(2*BT,K))
        wqS=tl.dot(wq,S,input_precision=PREC)  # [2BT,BV]
        wqS2=tl.reshape(wqS,(2,BT,BV))
        wS=tl.sum(tl.where((nb2[:,None,None]==0),wqS2,0.0),axis=0)
        qgS=tl.sum(tl.where((nb2[:,None,None]==1),wqS2,0.0),axis=0)
        v_i=u-wS
        o=qgS+tl.dot(Aqk,v_i,input_precision=PREC)
        t_idx=i_n*BT+offs_r; v_row=(i_b*T+t_idx)*HV+i_h*V
        tl.store(o_ptr+v_row[:,None]+offs_v[None,:],o.to(o_ptr.dtype.element_ty))
        kn=tl.dot(tl.trans(kng),v_i,input_precision=PREC)
        S=tl.exp(glast)[:,None]*(S+kn)

shape={"B":1,"T":4096,"H":8,"K":128,"V":128,"CHUNK_SIZE":64}
for k,v in shape.items(): setattr(reference,k,v)
init=reference.get_init_inputs(); inp=[t.to(device) for t in reference.get_inputs()]
q,k,v,g,beta=inp
B,T,H,K=q.shape; V=v.shape[-1]; BT=64; NT=T//BT; NBH=B*H*NT
def mk(dt,sz): return torch.empty(sz,device=device,dtype=dt)
w=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); u=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*V); Aqk=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*BT)
qg=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); kng=mk(torch.bfloat16,NBH*BT*K); glast=mk(torch.float32,NBH*K)
o=torch.empty_like(v)
from solution import _kda_rec_kernel
def rec_orig(): _kda_rec_kernel[(triton.cdiv(V,16),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=16,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=2)
def rec_comb(): rec_combined[(triton.cdiv(V,16),B*H)](w,u,Aqk,qg,kng,glast,o,B,T,H,NT=NT,BT=BT,K=K,V=V,BV=16,PREC="tf32",num_warps=4,num_stages=2)
print("orig:", round(tt.do_bench(rec_orig,warmup=10,rep=100)*1000), "us")
print("comb:", round(tt.do_bench(rec_comb,warmup=10,rep=100)*1000), "us")
EOF

tool
stdout (1254 chars)
1	"""Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) forward, chunk form — custom Triton kernels for
2	SM120 (RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell).
3	
4	Two-kernel split:
5	
6	  1) intra kernel  — grid (B*H*NT,).  One program per (b, h, chunk).
7	     Computes, per chunk (in-chunk cumsummed gate g_cs):
8	        k_g  = k * exp(g_cs);  k_ng = k * exp(-g_cs);  q_g = q * exp(g_cs)
9	        gram = k_g @ k_ng^T                        (decayed K-K gram)
10	        N    = beta_row * gram                     (strictly lower)
11	        Tinv = (I + N)^{-1} = I + sum_{k>=1} (-N)^k   (Neumann row-scan)
12	        A    = Tinv * beta_col
13	        w    = A @ k_g         ;  u = A @ v
14	        Aqk  = lower_incl_diag(q_g @ k_ng^T)
15	     Stores w, u, Aqk, q_g, k_ng, g_last (=g_cs[BT-1]) to HBM.
16	
17	  2) recurrence kernel — grid (V/BV, B*H).  Sequential over chunks.
18	        v_i = u - w @ S
19	        o   = q_g @ S + Aqk @ v_i
20	        S   = exp(g_last) * (S + k_ng^T @ v_i)
21	
22	Moving the (sequential, expensive) tril solve out of the recurrence into the
23	embarrassingly-parallel intra kernel restores occupancy on the 240-SM GPU.
24	"""
25	from __future__ import annotations
26	
27	import torch
28	import torch.nn as nn
29	import triton
30	import triton.language as tl
31
